lessons.title: std::mdspan: Syntax, Extents, and Layout Policies

background.title: std::span (C++20)
background.body: std::span<T> is a non-owning view over a contiguous array: it stores a pointer and a size without copying data. std::mdspan is its multi-dimensional generalization: a view over data with multiple dimensions. If std::span is a 1D view, std::mdspan is an N-dimensional view.

lessons.motivation: Multi-dimensional data — matrices, tensors, images — is ubiquitous in scientific computing, computer graphics, and machine learning. Yet C++ historically had no standard way to work with such structures: you had to use nested arrays with fixed sizes, write manual index arithmetic like data[i*cols + j], or pull in third-party libraries. Every project reinvented its own Matrix<T> or Tensor<T>. C++23 introduces std::mdspan — a standard non-owning multi-dimensional view that separates concerns: the user manages memory, while mdspan describes shape and traversal order. This enables writing generic algorithms that work over data in any layout without copying.
lessons.theory:
Arrays in memory are always one-dimensional. When we work with a 3×4 matrix, 12 numbers sit contiguously in memory. The question is only how to interpret that flat buffer as a two-dimensional structure. Before C++23, the standard had no answer — the developer was on their own. std::mdspan solves exactly this: it takes a pointer to existing data and describes how to read it as an N-dimensional array. mdspan does not own data — it is a pure view, analogous to std::span but for an arbitrary number of dimensions.

The basic syntax: std::mdspan(ptr, d0, d1, ...) creates a view over memory at ptr with extents d0 along the first dimension, d1 along the second, and so on. Element access uses multi-dimensional operator[]: m[i, j]. This is C++23 syntax — a true multi-dimensional operator[] taking multiple arguments in a single call, not the old trick of chaining m[i][j] through two separate calls. Key methods: m.extent(N) returns the size of the Nth dimension, m.rank() gives the number of dimensions, and m.size() gives the total element count.

The second template parameter of std::mdspan is extents, which describes the shape. Sizes can be static (known at compile time) or dynamic (known at runtime). std::extents<IndexType, E0, E1, ...> encodes specific sizes: std::extents<size_t, 2, 3> always means 2×3. For fully dynamic sizes there is the convenience alias std::dextents<IndexType, Rank> — for example, std::dextents<size_t, 2> for a matrix of arbitrary size. You can mix: std::extents<size_t, 2, std::dynamic_extent> pins the first dimension to 2 at compile time while the second is supplied at runtime. Static extents allow the compiler to generate more efficient code.

The third template parameter is the layout policy, which determines how a multi-dimensional index [i, j, ...] translates to an offset in the linear buffer. std::layout_right (the default, C-style, row-major): element [i, j] is at offset i*cols + j. Rows lie consecutively in memory. std::layout_left (Fortran-style, column-major): element [i, j] is at offset i + j*rows. Columns lie consecutively in memory. std::layout_stride lets you specify arbitrary strides per dimension through an explicit mapping object.

The choice of layout policy is critical for performance. If data is stored in row-major order but you iterate over columns (varying j in the outer loop), every step reads from a different cache line — a cache miss on every access. The rule: when using layout_right, vary the rightmost index fastest in your inner loop; when using layout_left, vary the leftmost index fastest. Linear algebra libraries like BLAS use Fortran (column-major) order, so when passing data between C++ and BLAS, layout_left is often used. std::mdspan lets you write algorithms that are parametric over the layout without rewriting the logic.

examples.title: Basic std::mdspan Usage
examples.description: Create a 2D view over a flat array, inspect shape via extent() and rank(), and modify an element through the multi-dimensional operator[].
examples.expectedOutput:
rank: 2
rows: 2, cols: 3
size: 6
Matrix:
1 2 3 
4 5 6 
After m[0,1] = 99:
1 99 3 
4 5 6
examples.explanation: std::mdspan(data.data(), 2, 3) creates the view: a pointer to the start of data, 2 rows, 3 columns. mdspan does not copy data. rank() returns 2 (two-dimensional), extent(0) and extent(1) give sizes along each axis. Access m[i, j] uses the C++23 multi-dimensional operator[] — a single call with two arguments, not m[i][j] which would be two separate operator[] calls. Writing m[0, 1] = 99 modifies the underlying data array directly, since mdspan is a view, not a copy.

examples.title: Static, Dynamic, and Mixed Extents
examples.description: Compare three ways to specify mdspan shape: fully static, fully dynamic via dextents, and mixed.
examples.expectedOutput:
Static 3x4: extent(0)=3, extent(1)=4
Dynamic 2x6: extent(0)=2, extent(1)=6
Mixed 2x?: extent(0)=2, extent(1)=6
d[1,2] = 9
examples.explanation: std::extents<size_t, 3, 4> encodes the 3×4 shape directly in the type — the compiler knows the sizes, so no runtime arguments are passed to the constructor. std::dextents<size_t, 2> is an alias for std::extents<size_t, std::dynamic_extent, std::dynamic_extent>: both sizes are runtime values passed to the constructor. The mixed form std::extents<size_t, 2, std::dynamic_extent> fixes the first dimension to 2 at compile time and accepts the second (6) at runtime. Element d[1, 2] in a 2×6 matrix is at offset 1*6+2=8, which is data[8]=9.

examples.title: Layout Policies: Row-Major, Column-Major, and Custom Strides
examples.description: Show how layout_right, layout_left, and layout_stride each interpret the same data buffer differently.
examples.explanation: Same buffer {1,2,3,4,5,6}, same extents<2,3>, three different layouts — three different matrices. layout_right: row 0 = elements 0..2 = {1,2,3}, row 1 = {4,5,6}. layout_left: column 0 = elements 0..1 = {1,2}, so [0,0]=1, [1,0]=2; column 1 = {3,4}, giving [0,1]=3, [1,1]=4; column 2 = {5,6}. layout_stride with strides [1, 2]: offset [i,j] = i*1 + j*2, so [0,0]=data[0]=1, [0,1]=data[2]=3, [0,2]=data[4]=5 — coincides with column-major for this example. layout_stride is used for submatrix views or data with non-standard alignment.

lessons.title: std::mdspan: submdspan, Accessor Policies, and Generic API
lessons.motivation: The first lesson showed how mdspan describes data shape and layout. But mdspan's real power shows up when working with sub-views, when writing library functions that accept any layout, and when extending the element-access policy. These three topics transform mdspan from a convenient syntax shortcut into the foundation for standard linear algebra in C++26.
lessons.theory:
std::submdspan is a function for extracting sub-views without copying data. The call std::submdspan(m, slice0, slice1, ...) returns a new mdspan that points into the same memory as m but describes only the selected fragment. Slice arguments control how each dimension is narrowed: std::full_extent keeps the dimension intact; a std::pair{start, end} specifies a half-open range [start, end); a single index removes the dimension entirely (rank decreases by 1, analogous to m[i] giving a row of a matrix); std::strided_slice{start, size, stride} picks an evenly-spaced subset. Unfortunately std::submdspan is not yet implemented in GCC trunk at the time of writing, so the examples in this lesson achieve the equivalent result via layout_stride — the same mechanism submdspan uses internally. Once submdspan is available in compilers, the syntax becomes significantly more compact.

The fourth template parameter of std::mdspan<T, Extents, Layout, Accessor> is the accessor policy. The default is std::default_accessor<T>, which simply returns ptr[offset]. An accessor is a class with several required members: type aliases element_type, reference, data_handle_type, and offset_policy; a method access(ptr, offset) -> reference that performs the actual element access; and a method offset(ptr, i) -> data_handle_type that advances the data handle. This lets you embed arbitrary access logic directly in the mdspan type: bounds checking in debug builds, atomic access via std::atomic_ref, alignment requirements for SIMD, or restrict annotations for HPC compilers. The accessor policy is invoked on every m[i, j] access — with no abstraction overhead because the compiler inlines the call.

The key pattern for using mdspan in library code is a template function that accepts mdspan with arbitrary parameters: template<class T, class E, class L, class A> void f(std::mdspan<T, E, L, A> m). Such a function works identically with row-major, column-major, strided, and any other representation without rewriting logic per layout. This is the fundamental advantage over raw-pointer APIs: instead of void f(double* data, int rows, int cols, int ld) — a function that knows nothing about the actual layout — you get a type-safe interface where the layout is encoded in the type and used by the compiler for correct addressing.

C++26 adds std::linalg — standard linear algebra algorithms (matrix multiplication, solving systems, decompositions) that accept mdspan parameters. This is why mdspan is a load-bearing component of the standard: it is not only convenient on its own, but serves as the foundation on which next-standard matrix operations are built. The std::linalg library covers most BLAS-level operations, works with any layout through parametric dependence on Layout and Accessor, and avoids manual stride management — which is today the primary source of bugs in numerical code.

examples.title: Sub-view via layout_stride (submdspan equivalent)
examples.description: Demonstrates extracting a sub-matrix without copying data. std::submdspan is not yet implemented in GCC trunk, so we use layout_stride directly — the same mechanism submdspan uses internally.
examples.explanation: The original 3×4 matrix is stored as 12 contiguous elements. The sub-matrix [rows 1-2, cols 1-3] starts at data[5] (element [1,1] = 6). Row stride is 4 (the original matrix width), column stride is 1. An mdspan with layout_stride({4,1}) starting at &data[5] produces exactly the same view that std::submdspan(m, std::pair{1,3}, std::pair{1,4}) would return. No data is copied — sub and m both look into the same data array.

examples.title: Generic functions — any layout, one implementation
examples.description: Template functions accepting std::mdspan with arbitrary parameters work identically for row-major, column-major, and any other layout.
examples.expectedOutput:
row-major sum: 21
col-major sum: 21
sums equal: true
2x2 row-major trace (1+4): 5
examples.explanation: mdspan_sum and mdspan_trace are parameterized on all four mdspan template parameters — T, E, L, A. That means one code path works with row-major, column-major, strided, and any custom accessor. The compiler instantiates the function separately for each combination and generates optimal code. The sum is layout-independent (21 = 1+2+3+4+5+6 in both cases). Tracing the 2×2 matrix returns data[0]+data[3] = 1+4 = 5 under row-major layout. Compare this to a raw-pointer API: there you would need to pass stride as a separate parameter, and the layout would be an implicit convention.

examples.title: Custom accessor policy: bounds checking
examples.description: Implement an accessor with bounds checking. It is embedded in the mdspan type and intercepts every element access — without changing any algorithm code.
examples.explanation: CheckedAccessor has three required components: type aliases (element_type, reference, data_handle_type, offset_policy), an access(ptr, offset) method — performs the actual element read, here with bounds checking — and an offset(ptr, i) method that advances the data handle. The mdspan constructor takes the data, a mapping (how the layout translates indices to an offset), and an accessor. When m[0, 1] is called, the compiler: (1) uses the mapping to compute the linear offset (here 1), (2) calls accessor.access(ptr, 1) — where our check runs. In a debug configuration this gives safety; in release the accessor is swapped for std::default_accessor and the check disappears — without touching algorithm code.
